ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for 7 Up Lemon; mapped to the closest parent carbonated beverage class FOODON_03301710.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for lemon-flavoured carbonated soft drink; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: High fructose corn syrup or sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 320 ml can of 7 Up Lemon, as commonly sold in Singapore via food delivery platforms, providing approximately 140 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Carbonated Beverage, Lemon-Lime, Regular (SR Legacy NDB 14148)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for regular lemon-lime carbonated soft drink (NDB 14148), the closest approved-source equivalent to 7 Up Lemon. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood listing (energy 43.75 kcal, carbohydrate 11.88 g, sugar 11.88 g, sodium 14.06 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy reference values for this beverage category and have been retained. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation for all standard lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks per USDA SR Legacy data. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source; it is referenced only as the origin of the input record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference values for regular sugar-sweetened lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks. The Sydney database reports GI values of approximately 63 for regular cola-type and lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks sweetened with glucose-fructose syrup or sucrose. No exact GI test result for the 7 Up Lemon product variant sold in Singapore was located in the Sydney database as of 2024. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 38.016) / 100 = 23.95, where carbohydrate per serving = 11.88 g/100 g × 320 g = 38.016 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.88 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid 7 Up Lemon entirely; the 11.88 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain that independently elevates blood pressure regardless of the beverage's relatively low sodium content (14 mg/100 g); substitute with plain sparkling water with fresh lemon or lime juice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.88 g sugar per 100 g and GI of approximately 63; a 320 ml can delivers approximately 38 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with zero dietary fibre to blunt glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid 7 Up Lemon; the 38 g sugar load per 320 ml can is entirely from rapidly absorbed simple sugars with no fibre buffer, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation and worsening insulin resistance over time; choose water, unsweetened sparkling water, or beverages sweetened with non-nutritive sweeteners under clinical guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased risk of cardiovascular events, dyslipidaemia, and visceral adiposity per AHA and WHO evidence reviews.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should eliminate 7 Up Lemon from their diet; habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption at this sugar concentration (11.88 g/100 g) is linked to elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased visceral fat — all independent cardiovascular risk factors; replace with water or unsweetened beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; beverage provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid 7 Up Lemon; the high refined sugar content (11.88 g/100 g) promotes inflammatory cytokine activity and the beverage provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients; replace with unsweetened green tea or plain water with fresh lemon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 7 Up Lemon is a lemon-lime flavoured carbonated soft drink produced by the 7 Up brand, sold globally and widely available in Singapore via food delivery platforms and convenience stores. It is a ready-to-drink, ultra-processed sugar-sweetened beverage consisting primarily of carbonated water, high fructose corn syrup or sugar, citric acid, natural lemon and lime flavours, sodium citrate, and sodium benzoate as a preservative. The drink is clear, effervescent, and delivers a crisp, sweet-tart lemon-lime taste with a light citrus aroma. It contains no protein, no fat, and no dietary fibre; its entire caloric content of approximately 43.75 kcal per 100 ml comes from 11.88 g of simple sugars per 100 ml. A standard 320 ml can provides approximately 140 kcal and 38 g of sugar. The glycaemic index is approximately 63 (medium), and the glycaemic load per 320 ml can is approximately 24, making it a significant glycaemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Clinically, 7 Up Lemon is contraindicated for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar load, absence of fibre, and pro-inflammatory potential. It is vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free. Common serving context in Singapore is as a standalone thirst-quenching beverage or as a mixer in non-alcoholic drinks.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of 7 Up Lemon?
Is 7 Up Lemon suitable for diabetics?
How much sugar is in 7 Up Lemon?
Can I drink 7 Up Lemon if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of 7 Up Lemon?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values from the GrabFood input listing are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for regular lemon-lime carbonated soft drink (the closest approved-source equivalent). Core macros, sugar, sodium, and energy are all internally consistent and cross-validated against the USDA SR Legacy reference. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation for all standard lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks per USDA SR Legacy data.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero — lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks contain no protein by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (lemon-lime carbonated beverage, NDB 14148). fat_g is naturally zero — lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks contain no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all naturally zero by product formulation; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — carbonated sugar-sweetened beverages contain no dietary fibre; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero — plant-derived beverage with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific 7 Up Lemon product variant sold in Singapore are not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lemon-lime carbonated soft drink (NDB 14148) as of 2024; the USDA SR Legacy entry reports negligible or not-determined values for these micronutrients in this beverage category. cultural.primary_cuisine and ethnic_tags in the input JSON incorrectly referenced 'The Noodle Place - Circular Road' (a restaurant name); this has been corrected to reflect the global/international origin of the 7 Up brand. GI value of 63 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for regular sugar-sweetened lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks; no exact GI test result for 7 Up Lemon specifically was located in the Sydney database as of 2024. foodon_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as no exact FoodOn term exists for lemon-lime carbonated soft drink; FOODON_03301710 (carbonated beverage) is the closest parent class.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name '7 Up Lemon Carbonated Soft Drink' is precise and unambiguous. Multilingual names provided in Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi satisfying the Singapore-market multilingual requirement for a beverage sold in Singapore.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (43.75), protein_g (0.0), fat_g (0.0), and carbohydrate_g (11.88) are all present and numeric. Zero values for protein and fat are justified in notes as naturally zero by product formulation, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 14148. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — an approved primary source. GrabFood is explicitly noted as the origin of the input record only, not as a nutrient source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (14.06), saturated_fat_g (0.0), and sugar_g (11.88) are all present as numeric values, plausible, and consistent with USDA SR Legacy reference for lemon-lime carbonated soft drink.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (63), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (23.95), and gi_source are all present. GI evidence is based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for regular sugar-sweetened lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks; estimation basis is fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes. GL calculation is shown explicitly: (63 × 38.016) / 100 = 23.95.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn mapped to FOODON_03301710 (carbonated beverage) with foodon_match_type set to 'closest_parent' and gap note provided. FoodEx2 code AN0KN (Carbonated soft drinks) is a defensible exact match. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, its formulation, taste and texture, clinical relevance for diabetes/hypertension/CVD/RA, key nutrients, GI/GL, and serving context in Singapore.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid Arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 14 entries covering alternate English names, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, romanized Chinese, and common descriptive variants. All values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Consistency check passed.

Multilingual naming covers Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for a Singapore-market product. foodon_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent'.
